landing.title: How to Train Your LLM
landing.subtitle: an interactive guide to building a language model from scratch

ch00.title: The Magic Trick
ch00.s01_text: You've probably used ChatGPT. You type something, it types back. Feels like magic, right?
ch00.s03_text: Impressive, right? But here's the thing — there's no magic here. Just math, data, and a clever trick called "predict the next word."
ch00.s04_text: Over the next chapters, we're going to build one of these from scratch. You'll see every piece, touch every gear.
ch00.s04_text2: By the end, you'll know exactly how it works — because you'll have built it yourself.

ch01.title: What Is Text, Really?
ch01.s01_text: So we want a machine to understand text. But here's the problem — computers don't understand letters. They only understand numbers.
ch01.s02_text: First, we split text into individual characters. Each one gets a number.
ch01.s02_btn: OK, but that's a LOT of tokens... →
ch01.s03_text: There's a smarter way: find common pairs and merge them. This is called Byte Pair Encoding (BPE). Try it — click the highlighted pair to merge!
ch01.s03_complete: Nice! You just built a tokenizer. Instead of {{charCount}} character tokens, we now have {{tokenCount}} tokens.
ch01.s03_btn: Cool! So each word is one token now? →
ch01.s04_text: You might think each word becomes one token. BUT real tokenization is much weirder...
ch01.s05_text: So tokenization is the first step. The model doesn't see words — it sees tokens. And how you split text into tokens matters a lot.
ch01.s05_text2: Next, we'll teach the model to predict which token comes next.

ch02.title: The Guessing Game
ch02.s01_text: Now that we have tokens, how does a model know what comes next? The simplest idea: just count. Look at what word came before, and guess the most likely next word.
ch02.s01_text2: This is called a bigram model. Let's see how well it does.
ch02.s03_text2: The bigram only looks at the very last word. You read the whole sentence. That's the difference — context.
ch02.s03_btn: So how do we give the model more context? →
ch02.s04_text: The bigram sees one word. BUT what if we could teach a model to see the whole sentence? To weigh how important each word is for predicting the next one?
ch02.s04_text2: That's exactly what we'll build in the next chapter.
ch02.s05_text: We need a mechanism that lets each token look at all the other tokens and decide which ones matter most.

ch03.title: Paying Attention
ch03.s01_text: Remember how the bigram only looked at one word? We need something better.
ch03.s01_text2: We need each word to look at EVERY other word and decide: which ones matter to me?
ch03.s02_text: Think of it like this: each word asks a Question — "what am I looking for?" Each word has a Key — "what do I contain?" And each word has a Value — "what info do I carry."
ch03.s02_text2: When a Question matches a Key, that word's Value gets passed along. This is called Attention.
ch03.s03_instruction: Which words should "{{word}}" pay attention to? Click them!
ch03.s03_score_perfect: Perfect! You found all the connections.
ch03.s04_text: You just played the role of an attention head. One head learns one pattern — like connecting pronouns to their subjects.
ch03.s04_text2: BUT language has so many relationships... position, grammar, meaning, coreference... We need MULTIPLE heads!
ch03.s05_instruction: Click each head to see what it learns:
ch03.s06_text: Multiple heads, each learning different patterns. Together they capture the rich web of relationships in language.
ch03.s06_text2: But attention is just one piece. Next, we'll assemble the full Transformer.

ch04.title: The Transformer
ch04.s01_text: We have attention — a way for tokens to talk to each other. But attention alone isn't enough.
ch04.s01_text2: We need to wrap it in a full architecture. This is: the Transformer.
ch04.s02_instruction: Drag each component into the correct position to build the Transformer pipeline:
ch04.s03_text: Excellent! Now let's see what happens when a token flows through this pipeline.
ch04.s05_text: One Transformer layer: Attention + FFN. Simple, right?
ch04.s05_text2: BUT one layer isn't powerful enough. What if we stack 12? 24? GPT-3 uses 96 layers! Each layer refines the representation further.
ch04.s05_btn: So how do we train this? →
ch04.s06_text: The Transformer is elegant: stack the same simple block over and over. Embedding → [Attention + FFN] × N → Output.
ch04.s06_text2: Now we know the architecture. But how do we actually TRAIN it? That's next — feeding the beast.

ch05.title: Feeding the Beast
ch05.s01_text: We have the architecture. Now we need to train it.
ch05.s01_text2: The recipe: show the model LOTS of text, make it predict the next token. When it guesses wrong, adjust the weights. Repeat billions of times.
ch05.s03_text: The loss number measures how surprised the model is. High loss = bad guesses. As training continues, loss goes down — the model gets better at predicting.
ch05.s04_text: More data + bigger model = lower loss. This is the scaling law — it's why companies keep building bigger models.
ch05.s04_text2: It reliably makes them better. GPT-3 has 175 billion parameters. GPT-4 is even bigger.
ch05.s05_prompt: Write me an email about the meeting:
ch05.s05_text: See the problem? It writes beautifully... but it doesn't FOLLOW INSTRUCTIONS. It learned to complete, not to obey.
ch05.s06_text: To turn this text-completion engine into a helpful assistant, we need a new training phase.
ch05.s06_text2: Welcome to Act II: Supervised Fine-Tuning.

ch06.title: The Obedient Student
ch06.s01_text: Great news — you've trained a language model! It can generate fluent text.
ch06.s01_text2: But try asking it a question... it just keeps completing text instead of answering. It learned to COMPLETE, not to CONVERSE.
ch06.s02_instruction: Which response is from the instruction-tuned (SFT) model?
ch06.s02_wrong: Not quite — the other one is the SFT model.
ch06.s03_text: You scored {{score}}/4. The difference is striking — same model, same weights, but with fine-tuning on instruction-response examples, it transforms from a text completer into a helpful assistant.
ch06.s04_text: SFT taught the model to follow instruction formats and imitate annotator style. BUT it's just copying patterns. It doesn't truly understand what makes a 'good' answer.
ch06.s04_text2: Different annotators might disagree on the best response. We need a more systematic way to define 'good'.
ch06.s05_text: To teach the model what 'good' means, we need to learn from human preferences.
ch06.s05_text2: But first, let's look at how SFT actually works in practice.

ch07.title: Teaching by Example
ch07.s01_text: SFT works by showing the model examples of good instruction-response pairs.
ch07.s01_text2: But someone has to CREATE those examples. That someone is a data annotator. Today, that's you.
ch07.s02_rank_instruction: Click the responses in order: best first, worst last.
ch07.s02_different: Interesting — you ranked differently. Here's why:
ch07.s03_text: Notice how some 'OK' responses were technically correct but didn't follow instructions well?
ch07.s03_text2: Data quality matters more than quantity. 1,000 high-quality examples can beat 100,000 sloppy ones.
ch07.s04_text: Now here's a tricky one. What's the 'best' answer to an ethical question?
ch07.s04_question: Three annotators, three different 'best' answers. Who's right?
ch07.s05_text: Exactly. You now have an obedient model. BUT different annotators disagree on what 'good' means.
ch07.s05_text2: SFT can only teach the model to mimic examples — it can't learn PREFERENCES. For that, we need a new approach: learning from human feedback.

ch08.title: What Do Humans Want?
ch08.s01_text: The model follows instructions now. But how do we teach it the DIFFERENCE between a good answer and a great one?
ch08.s01_text2: We collect human preferences. You pick which response is better. Let's build a preference dataset.
ch08.s02_different: Interesting choice — here's what most raters thought:
ch08.s03_text: Your preferences trained a reward model — a separate model that scores responses. Now the reward model can judge quality automatically.
ch08.s03_btn: Sounds great! What could go wrong? →
ch08.s04_text: Well... look at these two responses. The reward model scores them:
ch08.s04_text2: The model found a shortcut — enthusiasm and length trick the reward model into giving high scores.
ch08.s05_text: The reward model can be fooled. We need guardrails — a way to improve the model while preventing it from going off the rails.
ch08.s05_text2: That's what the final step is for: reinforcement learning with constraints.

ch09.title: The Alignment Game
ch09.s01_text: We have a reward model. Now we use reinforcement learning to optimize the language model.
ch09.s01_text2: But we can't just maximize the reward, or the model will find shortcuts to hack it.
ch09.s02_text: The solution: a leash. We let the model improve, but penalize it for straying too far from its original behavior.
ch09.s02_text2: This leash is called KL divergence — it measures how different the new model is from the original. Too different? Pull it back.
ch09.s03_instruction: Adjust the sliders to find the right balance:
ch09.s03_warning_helpful: Warning: model is too eager to help — might give dangerous advice!
ch09.s03_warning_safe: Warning: model is too cautious — refuses to help with reasonable requests!
ch09.s03_balanced: Good balance! The model is helpful while staying safe.
ch09.s04_text: Too helpful → reckless. Too safe → useless. The art of alignment is finding the right balance.
ch09.s04_text2: And that balance isn't fixed — it depends on context, culture, and what we want AI to be.
ch09.s05_text: We trained a helpful, safe, honest model. BUT this is just the beginning.
ch09.s05_text2: Alignment is an ongoing, open problem. The values we encode, the tradeoffs we make — these are choices that shape how AI affects the world.
ch09.s06_text: You started with a magic trick. Now you've seen every step:
ch09.s06_pipeline: Tokenization → Embeddings → Attention → Transformers → Pretraining → SFT → RLHF
ch09.s06_text2: There's no magic — just math, data, and human choices.
ch09.s06_text3: And now you know how it works, because you built it yourself.

ch08.s01_text2: We collect human preferences. You pick which response is better. Let's build a preference dataset.
